nodes.description: Blue sclerae are a classic extraskeletal sign of osteogenesis imperfecta type I.

evidence.reference_title: Type I osteogenesis imperfecta: a nonfunctional allele for pro alpha 1 (I) chains of type I procollagen.
evidence.snippet: Type I osteogenesis imperfecta (OI) is a dominantly inherited disease characterized clinically by bone fractures during childhood, blue sclerae, and frequent hearing loss accompanied by a decreased content of type I collagen in bone and skin.

nodes.description: Chronic or recurrent bone pain is reported by adults with OI type I and is among the most frequently cited unmet clinical concerns in this population.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002653 | Bone pain | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Incorporating the patient perspective in the study of rare bone disease.
evidence.snippet: Other areas included pain, gastrointestinal problems,

nodes.description: Easy bruising is a recognised extraskeletal feature of OI type I, more prominent than in type IV. It reflects deficient type I collagen in skin and subcutaneous connective tissue.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000978 | Bruising susceptibility | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.reference_title: Osteogenesis imperfecta with dominant inheritance and normal sclerae.
evidence.snippet: In Type 4 the first fracture more commonly occurs at birth, dentinogenesis imperfecta is more frequent than in Type 1 and bruising and nose-bleeds are less common.

nodes.description: Dentinogenesis imperfecta can occur in a subset of children with osteogenesis imperfecta type I.

evidence.reference_title: Dentinogenesis imperfecta in children with osteogenesis imperfecta: a clinical and ultrastructural study.
evidence.snippet: RESULTS: A total of ten patients had abnormal discolourations referable to DI: four patients were affected by OI type I, three patients by OI type III, and three patients by OI type IV.

nodes.description: Progressive hearing impairment usually begins in adulthood, with onset most often in the second to fourth decades.

evidence.reference_title: How common is hearing impairment in osteogenesis imperfecta?
evidence.snippet: It was found that the most common age of onset was in the second, third and fourth decades of life. At the age of 50 approximately 50 per cent of the patients had symptoms of hearing impairment; over the next 20 years there was little further increase. Differences were shown between patients with different clinical types of osteogenesis imperfecta as delineated in the Sillence classification; hearing loss was significantly less common in the type IV disease than in the type I disorder.

nodes.description: Increased sweating is reported in a substantial minority of OI patients. It arises from altered connective-tissue and autonomic thermoregulatory responses related to type I collagen deficiency.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000975 | Hyperhidrosis | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Joint laxity is an extraskeletal feature of type I collagen deficiency, arising from reduced collagen content in ligaments and joint capsules. Symptomatic lax joints are managed with orthotics and mobility aids.

evidence.snippet: Mobility aids including orthotics to stabilize lax joints.

nodes.description: Adults with OI type I have mildly reduced stature, averaging approximately 8–10 cm shorter than the general population (height Z-score approximately −1.1), though near-normal stature is preserved relative to the more severe OI types caused by structural collagen mutations.

evidence.reference_title: Skeletal clinical characteristics of osteogenesis imperfecta caused by haploinsufficiency mutations in COL1A1.
evidence.snippet: The average final height Z-score was -1.1, representing a deficit of 8 to 10 cm compared to the general population.

nodes.description: Reduced bone mass meeting the threshold for osteoporosis is documented in adults with OI type I, reflecting the underlying 50% deficit in type I collagen production.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000939 | Osteoporosis | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Skeletal clinical characteristics of osteogenesis imperfecta caused by haploinsufficiency mutations in COL1A1.
evidence.snippet: year, one-half of which affected the tibia/fibula. Long-bone fracture rate was

nodes.description: Hearing loss in OI type I is typically progressive, beginning in the second to fourth decade and advancing over subsequent decades.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001730 | Progressive hearing impairment | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: How common is hearing impairment in osteogenesis imperfecta?
evidence.snippet: It was found that the most common age of onset was in the second, third and fourth decades of life. At the age of 50 approximately 50 per cent of the patients had symptoms of hearing impairment; over the next 20 years there was little further increase.

nodes.description: Recurrent long-bone fractures are a core skeletal manifestation, and fracture rate declines with age.

evidence.reference_title: Skeletal clinical characteristics of osteogenesis imperfecta caused by haploinsufficiency mutations in COL1A1.
evidence.snippet: The average rate of long-bone fracture (femur, tibia/fibula, humerus, radius/ulna) in pediatric patients was 0.62 fractures per year, one-half of which affected the tibia/fibula. Long-bone fracture rate was negatively associated with age and lumbar spine areal bone mineral density.

nodes.description: Mild scoliosis occurs in a substantial minority of pediatric patients.

evidence.reference_title: Skeletal clinical characteristics of osteogenesis imperfecta caused by haploinsufficiency mutations in COL1A1.
evidence.snippet: Scoliosis was present in about 30% of pediatric patients but the Cobb angle was <30 degrees in all cases.

nodes.description: Null mutations in COL1A1 (nonsense, frameshift, splice site mutations causing nonsense-mediated mRNA decay) result in ~50% reduction in type I collagen production. The collagen that is produced has normal structure, explaining the milder phenotype compared to dominant-negative structural mutations.

evidence.reference_title: Osteogenesis imperfecta type I is commonly due to a COL1A1 null allele of type I collagen.
evidence.snippet: Dermal fibroblasts from most individuals with osteogenesis imperfecta (OI) type I produce about half the normal amount of type I procollagen, as a result of decreased synthesis of one of its constituent chains, pro alpha 1 (I).

evidence.reference_title: Skeletal clinical characteristics of osteogenesis imperfecta caused by haploinsufficiency mutations in COL1A1.
evidence.snippet: COL1A1 haploinsufficiency mutations lead to the mildest form of osteogenesis imperfecta (OI), OI type I.

nodes.description: Vertebral compression fractures are frequent in growing patients with COL1A1 haploinsufficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Skeletal clinical characteristics of osteogenesis imperfecta caused by haploinsufficiency mutations in COL1A1.
evidence.snippet: Vertebral compression fractures were observed in 71% of the 58 pediatric patients who had lateral spine radiographs.

nodes.description: Wormian (sutural) bones are accessory intrasutural ossification centres visible on skull radiographs. They are a recognised radiographic finding in the OI spectrum, including in milder type I cases.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002645 | Wormian bones | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.reference_title: Monitoring Skull Base Abnormalities in Children with Osteogenesis Imperfecta.
